title: Self-Worth
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Self-Worth
summary: Scripture grounds human value not in achievement or appearance but in being created in the image of God and personally known by the Creator before birth.
body:
The biblical case for human worth begins on page one. Humanity alone is made in the image of God — a designation that confers inherent dignity regardless of ability, productivity, or social status [verse:gen.1.27]. This is not earned worth; it is given worth, embedded in the act of creation itself.

Psalm 139 takes this further into the personal. The psalmist marvels that God knit him together in his mother's womb and declares himself 'fearfully and wonderfully made' [verse:ps.139.13-14]. This is not self-esteem rhetoric — it is theological precision. Human value derives from the care and intentionality of the Maker.

Jesus reinforced this by placing extraordinary value on individuals whom society discarded. He touched lepers, spoke to Samaritan women, and told parables about shepherds who leave ninety-nine sheep to recover one [verse:luke.15.4-7]. The math makes no economic sense. That is the point.

Paul argues that believers are God's workmanship — the Greek word is poiema, from which we get 'poem' — created in Christ Jesus for good works prepared in advance [verse:eph.2.10]. You are not an accident or an afterthought. You are a deliberate composition.

When self-worth wobbles, Scripture redirects attention from the mirror to the Maker.

verses.annotation: The foundational statement of human dignity: made in the image of God, male and female.
God's intimate involvement in forming each person — fearfully and wonderfully made.
The parable of the lost sheep — God's disproportionate pursuit of the individual.
Believers as God's workmanship (poiema) — a crafted masterpiece with prepared purpose.
God declares Israel precious and honored in his sight — valued by divine affection.
Jesus argues from lesser to greater: if God tracks sparrows, how much more does he value you.

scholars.quote: Sarna explains that the 'image of God' in Genesis 1:27 is not about physical resemblance but about humanity's unique capacity to represent God on earth — a status shared equally by every human being regardless of rank.

Goldingay reads Psalm 139 not primarily as a comfort text but as an assertion of God's comprehensive knowledge — the psalmist's worth is established by being fully known, not by being fully understood by others.

Keener highlights that Jesus' interactions with marginalized people in Luke's Gospel were socially subversive — each encounter was a public declaration that the person's value was not determined by cultural gatekeepers.
scholars.source: The IVP Bible Background Commentary: NT (Keener, 1993)
